title: The Warden's Name Game
statement_md:
Our hostel warden keeps a thick register with every student's name in it. During evening roll call, when he gets bored, he plays a little game: he reads a name letter by letter, then reads it again **backwards**. If both readings sound exactly the same, he grins and marks a little star next to that name.

You are given the names he reads out. For each name, tell him whether it deserves a star — that is, whether the name reads the same forwards and backwards.

Every name is a single word in lowercase English letters. Print `YES` if the name reads the same both ways, otherwise print `NO`.
input_format_md:
The first line contains a single integer `T` — the number of names.

Each of the next `T` lines contains one name — a single word of lowercase English letters.
output_format_md: For each name, print `YES` or `NO` on its own line.
constraints_md:
- `1 ≤ T ≤ 10`
- `1 ≤ length of each name ≤ 10^4`
- each name contains lowercase English letters (`a`–`z`) only

reference_solution.python:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    t = int(data[0])
    out = []
    for i in range(1, t + 1):
        s = data[i]
        out.append("YES" if s == s[::-1] else "NO")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out) + "\n")

main()

hints: You do not need to build anything new — just compare letters. Which two letters must match first for the name to read the same both ways?
Keep two pointers: one at the first letter, one at the last. Compare, then move both inwards. If every pair matches before they cross, print YES; the first mismatch means NO.
